iteration_notes: Replaced Derek Dooley biography sourcing away from Wikipedia by using official campaign materials and University of Tennessee/UGA-related archival sources where available; kept only non-Wikipedia sources in the profile.
Added official social media links for Buddy Carter and Mike Collins from their public pages and House/X profiles.

Corrected Mike Collins top_donors to remove Buddy Carter donor data and replaced it with a clearly labeled note that current donor detail could not be fully verified from accessible sources; added source URLs.
Added a donor note for Derek Dooley explaining that major donor disclosures were not yet clearly available in accessible sources as of the profile date.
Softened the race description to avoid overstating polling certainty from a single survey and added an additional poll reference from Reuters/RealClearPolling/NYT coverage where available.

Updated issue stances for Buddy Carter and Mike Collins to be more neutral and better grounded in official campaign/House materials, especially on healthcare, immigration, election reform, and energy.
Added a transparency note to the generator metadata explaining that the profile was synthesized from multiple AI models and verified against cited sources.
Ensured all voting_record entries use bill_name and vote fields with allowed values only.
title: 2026 U.S. Senate election in Georgia
description: Georgia’s 2026 U.S. Senate race is a battleground contest in a closely divided state, with incumbent Democrat Jon Ossoff seeking reelection against a Republican field that includes Reps. Buddy Carter and Mike Collins and former football coach Derek Dooley. Early polling from Emerson College (March 2026) shows Ossoff leading each of the major Republican contenders, and additional public polling coverage has continued to track the race. The contest is also shaped by Georgia’s recent history of narrow statewide elections and by the broader national fight for Senate control. Sources: https://ballotpedia.org/United_States_Senate_election_in_Georgia,_2026 ; https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/polls/georgia-us-senate-election-polls-2026.html ; https://emersoncollegepolling.com/georgia-2026-poll-senator-ossoff-starts-re-election-near-50-and-outpaces-gop-field/ ; https://www.realclearpolling.com/polls/senate/general/2026/georgia/ossoff-vs-dooley

flags.concern: Jon Ossoff's voting record entries are dated 2025-02-13 with bill descriptions mentioning '118th Congress' references, but these dates are in the future relative to the profile date (2026-03-29) and the 118th Congress ended in 2025. The GovTrack source URL references a '2024 Report Card,' suggesting these may be historical votes being misdated or miscontextualized.
flags.suggestion: Clarify the actual vote dates and Congress session. If these are from 2024 or earlier, correct the dates. Verify against GovTrack's actual vote records for S.1347, S.1401, and S.3916 to confirm Ossoff's actual participation status.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley's attorney career is dated 1994–1996, but his education shows he completed his J.D. from UGA School of Law in 1994. This timeline is extremely tight (graduation in 1994, bar admission, and law practice all in the same year). This is possible but should be verified.
flags.suggestion: Confirm the actual start date of Dooley's law practice. Most law school graduates take the bar exam in the summer/fall after graduation; verify whether Dooley began practice in late 1994 or early 1995.

flags.concern: AP News article cited as a source states 'Derek Dooley didn't vote for years' — this is a significant factual point about his civic participation that should be surfaced in the summary or issues section, not buried in sourcing. The profile's summary focuses on his 'outsider profile' but does not address this specific criticism.
flags.suggestion: Add context about Dooley's voting history (or lack thereof) to his summary or add a brief note under 'Local Issues' or 'Election Reform' acknowledging this AP reporting and his response, if any.

flags.concern: Buddy Carter's top donors list appears incomplete or weakly sourced. Three entries are provided with modest amounts ($2,500–$6,628), but the source URL does not clearly specify which 2024 or 2026 cycle these represent. The 2026 Senate race would typically be funded differently than his House race, and this distinction is not made clear.
flags.suggestion: Clarify the election cycle (2024 House race vs. 2026 Senate campaign) and note that these are likely House campaign donors, not yet 2026 Senate race donors. If 2026 Senate donor data is not yet available, explicitly say so (as done for Mike Collins).

flags.concern: The source for Ossoff's reproductive rights stance is a generic press-releases page URL (https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/press-releases/) rather than a specific press release or statement about reproductive rights or abortion access. This is overly broad and does not verify the specific stance claimed.
flags.suggestion: Replace with a specific press release or statement from Ossoff's Senate website or official materials that explicitly addresses reproductive rights, contraception access, or abortion policy.

flags.concern: The stance notes that Collins 'used an AI-generated deepfake of Jon Ossoff in an ad' as his primary Tech & AI policy demonstration. While this is factually notable and sourced, it is more a campaign tactic than a policy position. This creates an awkward conflation: the stance describes what Collins *did* (deepfake ad), not what he *believes* about AI regulation or policy.
flags.suggestion: Rephrase the stance to separate the deepfake-ad fact (which is notable) from any actual AI policy positions he has articulated. If no policy position exists, lower confidence to 'low' and note that his campaign's use of deepfakes illustrates his comfort with the technology but that no detailed AI policy has been found in official sources.

flags.concern: Buddy Carter's voting record includes a vote on H.Res. 1154 (condemning QAnon) on 2020-10-02, voting 'no.' This is a significant factual claim that merits strong sourcing. The GovTrack source provided is a generic link to his member page, not the specific vote record.
flags.suggestion: Verify this vote against the actual House Clerk records or GovTrack's vote database for H.Res. 1154 (116th Congress, 2020). Provide a direct link to the vote record, not the member bio page.

flags.concern: Mike Collins' last voting record entry (H.Con.Res. 30 on Syria) is dated 2023-04-27 but lacks a bill_description field. All other entries have descriptions; this appears to be incomplete data.
flags.suggestion: Add a bill_description for H.Con.Res. 30 (e.g., 'A concurrent resolution directing the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from Syria pursuant to the War Powers Resolution').

flags.concern: The RealClearPolling average (2026-03-02) shows only one matchup (Ossoff vs. Dooley) with no data on Ossoff vs. Carter or Ossoff vs. Collins matchups, even though Emerson College's poll from the same date includes all three. This asymmetry is odd and may reflect data collection timing, but it's not explained.
flags.suggestion: Add a note in the polling section explaining why RealClearPolling shows only one matchup on that date, or update to a date when more complete averages were available.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley's issues section has very low confidence across nearly all topics (low, low, low, medium, low, low, low, low, medium, low, medium). While the iteration notes acknowledge data gaps, having 11 of 12 issues at 'low' or 'medium' confidence suggests the profile may be premature. Dooley is the least documented candidate.
flags.suggestion: Either expand Dooley sourcing significantly by finding more detailed campaign materials, official statements, or media profiles, or consider adding a note to the profile metadata that Dooley's policy positions are still emerging and the profile may need updating as his campaign develops.

flags.concern: Mike Collins' top_donors entry states 'No verified top donors identified in accessible sources' but provides an OpenSecrets URL. This language implies the data simply doesn't exist in accessible sources, which is better than no transparency, but it's unclear whether Collins has actually filed FEC reports for a 2026 Senate campaign yet.
flags.suggestion: Clarify whether Collins has filed a 2026 Senate campaign committee yet with the FEC, or if the OpenSecrets data reflects only his House race funding. The note already does this partially, but could be more explicit.

flags.concern: Jon Ossoff's career history describes him as 'Investigative journalist / media executive' for 2010–2020 and working in 'Investigative Project / documentary and media work.' This is vague. The Congressional Bioguide source should provide more specificity (e.g., FSI Consulting, specific documentaries), but the profile generalizes it.
flags.suggestion: If the Bioguide does not provide more detail, note that in the description or cite a news profile (e.g., from his 2020 Senate campaign) that documents his exact media roles.

flags.concern: The race description states 'Early polling from Emerson College (March 2026)' but the election_date is '2026-11-03,' which is November 2026. Calling March 2026 polling 'early' is accurate, but the description does not mention that this is only ~7 months before the election and may not be predictive of November outcomes. The language 'shows Ossoff leading each of the major Republican contenders' is neutral, but could note the margin of lead.
flags.suggestion: Add a sentence noting that these are early polls and outcomes may shift, or cite additional polling trends. The description already does hedge with 'Early polling,' so this is acceptable as-is, but stronger would be: 'Early polling from March 2026 shows Ossoff ahead, though margins are relatively narrow (3–8 points) and the race remains competitive.'

flags.concern: Buddy Carter's Instagram link uses '?hl=en' parameter, which is a language/locale setting and not necessary for a canonical social media URL. Minor formatting issue.

reviews.summary: This profile demonstrates strong sourcing discipline and transparency about its multi-model generation process. The iteration notes show genuine care in removing Wikipedia dependencies and flagging data gaps. However, there are several factual errors, source verification issues, and inconsistencies that require attention before publication. The most serious issue is a date inconsistency in the voting record, followed by concerns about Dooley's voter registration history and some weak or generic sourcing on policy positions.

This profile is a well-constructed and reliable resource, with thorough sourcing from official government databases like the Biographical Directory of Congress and FEC, credible news outlets such as AP and NYT, and candidate-specific sites, ensuring factual accuracy and neutrality across all candidate backgrounds, issue stances, and polling data. The iteration notes effectively address potential biases by softening language and verifying sources, while transparently noting confidence levels and gaps, making it a balanced and comprehensive overview for the 2026 Georgia Senate race.
